straight_beat.name: Walt Kieniewicz
straight_beat.column:
The Cubs won yesterday. That’s what the box score says. A 7-6 decision over the Pirates, with Swanson singled home two in the seventh, Imanaga went six clean innings, and the Cubs beat the Pirates 4-2 at Wrigley. Record stands at 7-5. Wait, no—hold on. The record is 7-8, 2.0 games behind in the NL Central. That’s what the box score says.  

The win was a reminder that this team can still string together moments of competence. Swanson, the rookie, continued his surge with two RBI singles, and Imanaga, the rookie righty, held the Pirates to two runs over six innings. Those are the numbers. The problem is, those numbers don’t translate to a winning

pessimist.name: Tony Gedeski
pessimist.column:
The Cubs lost again. Another loss. Another step closer to the abyss. The Pirates handed them their eighth defeat of the season, and now they’re 7-8, 2.0 games behind in the NL Central. You know what’s funny? The same old story. The same old script. The same old Ricketts gonna Ricketts.  

You spend a billion on the marquee and forget to build a bullpen. Every Cubs summer is the same jazz and the same hangover. They signed star power, sure—Anthony Rizzo, Jason Heyward, and a bunch of other names that sound like they’re from a playoff roster. But what’s the point if the rest of the team is still a dumpster fire? The bullpen’s still a disaster. The middle of the order’s still a joke. And the front office? Oh, they’re still busy pretending they’re doing something.  

Look, I get it. Ricketts wants to be the savior of the Cubs. He wants to be the guy who finally brings the title back to Chicago. But instead of building a team, he’s just throwing money at the problem like it’s a buffet. They signed free agents to fill the void, but the void’s still there. The offense’s still inconsistent.
